Title: Low-acrylamide French fries and potato chips

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Asparagine synthetase family; N-terminal glutaminase domain (cd00712)
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes ATP-dependent conversion of aspartate to asparagine using glutamine as amide donor, producing glutamate
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Asparagine formation branch of amino acid/nitrogen metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key enzyme in asparagine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Undesirable food-processing contaminant with potential health concerns
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Heat-processed potato tuber products (French fries, potato chips)
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in tubers; silencing intended to be tuber-specific while limiting effects in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in tubers of Ranger Russet and reduced in intragenic lines
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Potato Gbss promoter (tuber-specific); potato Agp promoter (tuber-specific)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing decreases tuber free asparagine by up to about 95% and strongly reduces acrylamide formation in fries and chips
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. StAs1 was identified as one of two potato asparagine synthetase genes targeted for tuber-specific silencing. 2. Co-silencing with StAs2 markedly reduced free asparagine accumulation in tubers, with up to 20-fold reduction. 3. Lower tuber asparagine led to much lower acrylamide in processed products, in some fries to about 5% of control, without obvious yield or tuber-shape penalty under glasshouse conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; intragenic RNA silencing; RT-PCR; RNA gel blot; HPLC amino acid profiling; acrylamide quantification in processed products; yield/phenotype assessment
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cold storage at 4°C increased reducing sugars before fry processing; reconditioning at 15°C before chip processing
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Near-complete suppression may reduce total free amino acids in some lines; possible field stress sensitivity discussed but not tested; constitutive silencing predicted to impair growth
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for breeding/engineering low-acrylamide potato varieties via tuber-specific down-regulation; may need stacking with reduced cold-induced sweetening traits for chip applications
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Silencing was achieved through an inverted-repeat construct carrying fragments from both StAs1 and StAs2; pSIM1256 using Agp+Gbss promoters was more effective than pSIM1151 using two Gbss promoters

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Asparagine synthetase family; N-terminal glutaminase domain (cd00712)
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes ATP-dependent conversion of aspartate to asparagine using glutamine as amide donor, producing glutamate
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Asparagine formation branch of amino acid/nitrogen metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key enzyme in asparagine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Undesirable food-processing contaminant with potential health concerns
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Heat-processed potato tuber products (French fries, potato chips)
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Weakly expressed in untransformed tubers but detectable; reduced or undetectable in most intragenic tubers
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Measured in tubers by semi-quantitative RT-PCR and RNA gel blot; suppressed in most transformed lines
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Potato Gbss promoter (tuber-specific); potato Agp promoter (tuber-specific)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing decreases tuber free asparagine by up to about 95% and strongly reduces acrylamide formation in fries and chips
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. StAs2 was cloned from potato tubers and jointly targeted with StAs1 for silencing. 2. Reduced StAs2 transcript levels were correlated with suppressed StAs1 expression and sharply decreased tuber asparagine. 3. Co-silencing greatly lowered acrylamide formation in processed potato products while maintaining tuber yield and quality in tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; intragenic RNA silencing; RT-PCR; RNA gel blot; HPLC amino acid profiling; acrylamide quantification in processed products; yield/phenotype assessment
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cold storage at 4°C and tuber reconditioning at 15°C influenced acrylamide outcomes through sugar levels
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Some lines with extreme asparagine reduction showed lower total free amino acids; possible field stress sensitivity discussed but not tested
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Core target for engineering low-asparagine, low-acrylamide potatoes; likely best deployed with additional sugar-management traits in chip varieties
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: pSIM1256 was more effective than a dual-Gbss promoter construct; residual asparagine may come from incomplete silencing or transport from leaves
